chunk_summary: Guidelines for outpatient SAM management in India emphasize escalation criteria, addressing defaulting risks, and managing discordant anthropometry for safe treatment and discharge decisions.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for escalating SAM cases from outpatient to inpatient care.
Identify strategies to reduce defaulting in outpatient CMAM programs in India.
Learn how to manage discordant anthropometric measures during enrollment and discharge.
Incorporate follow-up and counseling techniques to improve adherence and safety.
key_facts: Escalation to inpatient care is required for danger signs such as oedema, persistent anorexia, or severe dehydration.
High defaulting rates in India are linked to travel difficulties and weak community referrals.
Frontline workers like ASHA reduce defaulting risks through follow-up and case tracing.
MUAC and WHZ identify different risk groups; both should be documented at baseline and discharge.
Discharge decisions should not rely on a single marker; both MUAC and WHZ must normalize for safe discharge.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Large Indian program experience has shown high defaulting when travel is difficult or when community referral is weak; referrals by frontline workers such as ASHA reduce default risk.
document_key_topics: Escalation criteria for SAM outpatient programs
chunk_heading: SEVERE ACUTE MALNUTRITION (SAM) MANAGEMENT;OUTPATIENT CMAM IMPLEMENTATION IN INDIA;OUTPATIENT ESCALATION DEFAULTING AND DISCORDANT ANTHROPOMETRY DECISIONS;
